food__drink_hero_application.box_1_the_f.answer:
I am the owner-chef of The Green Fork Bistro, a 32-cover neighbourhood restaurant in Worksop that focuses on seasonal, locally sourced food and low-waste cooking. I am applying for the North Notts Food & Drink Hero award because my work over the last five years has centred on three things: championing local producers, making good food accessible to everyone in our community, and building a genuinely sustainable, ethical business that looks after both people and the planet.

After training and working in Nottingham and Sheffield, I came back to North Notts in 2019 to open The Green Fork on Newcastle Avenue, ten minutes’ walk from where I grew up. From day one, I committed to sourcing at least 80% of our ingredients from within a 40-mile radius. Today, we regularly buy from over 18 local suppliers, including Moss Valley Farm (pork), Welbeck Farm Shop (dairy and charcuterie), Starkey’s Fruit (apples and soft fruit) and a small group of allotment growers in Worksop who supply us with surplus produce through a scheme we set up called "Plot to Plate." I personally visit these suppliers, share their stories on our menus and social media, and invite them to our quarterly producer evenings where customers meet the people behind their food.

Community has always been at the heart of what I do. During the pandemic, when we had to close the restaurant, we converted our kitchen into a community meal hub. Working with Bassetlaw Food Bank and a local church, we cooked and distributed over 7,000 free or pay-what-you-can meals between April 2020 and March 2021, focusing on older residents living alone and families whose children would normally receive free school meals. We used donations, surplus produce, and our own savings to make it happen. Even after reopening, we kept a weekly "Community Table" night: a three-course set menu at cost price, where people can discreetly pay what they can afford. No one is turned away, and regular customers often overpay to cover others.

Sustainability and ethical practice are not marketing buzzwords for us; they are built into how we operate. We reduced our food waste by over 70% in three years by designing root-to-leaf and nose-to-tail menus, preserving gluts from local growers, and training the team to use trimmings creatively (for example, carrot tops become pesto, cauliflower leaves are fermented, and stale bread becomes breadcrumbs and desserts). Any unavoidable food waste is composted in partnership with a local community garden, which in turn grows herbs and salad leaves we buy back. We moved to 100% renewable electricity, switched to refillable cleaning products from a Mansfield-based supplier, and eliminated nearly all single-use plastics in front-of-house.

I am also passionate about skills and opportunities for young people in North Notts. We currently have two apprentices from North Notts College, and I run free quarterly "Intro to Kitchen Skills" workshops for 14–18-year-olds who are curious about hospitality but might not see it as a viable career. Several of our current team members came through these workshops. I make a point of paying real Living Wage as a minimum, offering flexible hours to accommodate childcare and studies, and involving the whole team in menu development so that everyone feels ownership of what we serve.

What makes me proudest is when customers tell us that The Green Fork feels like "their" place – whether they’re celebrating anniversaries, coming in alone with a book, or joining our monthly "Supper & Story" nights where we pair dishes with stories about the farmers and growers who made them possible. I’m not a celebrity chef, and we’re not a flashy restaurant, but I believe that consistent, everyday commitment to good food, fairness, and community is exactly what the North Notts Food & Drink Hero award is about. I would be honoured if our work, and the work of the many local producers we support, could be recognised through this award.

food__drink_hero_application.box_2_suppo.answer:
Qualifications and Training:
- Level 3 Professional Cookery, New College Nottingham (2010)
- Level 2 Food Safety & Hygiene for Catering (renewed 2024)
- Level 2 Allergy Awareness in Catering (2023)
- Short course in Sustainable Menu Planning, Sheffield Hallam University (2021)
- First Aid at Work certificate (valid until 2027)

Awards and Recognitions:
- Finalist, Nottinghamshire Best Newcomer Restaurant (2021) – The Green Fork Bistro shortlisted in first year of eligibility.
- Winner, Bassetlaw Green Business Award (2023) – recognised for our low-waste approach, local sourcing, and community meal initiative.
- Highly Commended, North Notts Business Women’s Awards – Entrepreneur of the Year (2022).

Press and Media Coverage:
- "The Little Bistro Making a Big Difference" – feature article in the Worksop Guardian (May 2023) highlighting our community meal programme and partnership with Bassetlaw Food Bank.
- Profile in Nottinghamshire Live food & drink section (October 2022) focusing on our seasonal tasting menu and commitment to local producers.
- Interview on BBC Radio Nottingham (lunchtime show, February 2024) discussing rising food costs, how we keep our community menu affordable, and tips for reducing food waste at home.
- Mentioned in "48 Hours in North Notts" travel piece in a regional lifestyle magazine, recommending The Green Fork Bistro as a must-visit independent restaurant in Worksop.

Social Media and Online Presence:
- Instagram (@greenforkbistro): ~3,800 followers as of February 2026. We post daily behind-the-scenes stories, supplier visits, and short videos showing how to cook simple seasonal dishes at home. Our most-watched reel (over 25k views) is a tutorial on making leek and potato soup using only locally grown ingredients.
- Facebook (The Green Fork Bistro): ~2,400 followers. Used to promote community nights, charity events, and last-minute table availability. We regularly share posts from local producers to amplify their reach.
- Twitter/X (@greenforkbistro): Smaller following (~900), used mainly for industry conversations, sharing sustainability tips, and engaging with local councils and food networks.
- TripAdvisor: Consistently ranked in the top 5 restaurants in Worksop for the past three years, with an average rating of 4.8/5 from over 260 reviews. Common themes in reviews include friendly service, creative use of local ingredients, and welcoming atmosphere for solo diners and families.

Community and Charity Involvement:
- Bassetlaw Food Bank: Ongoing partnership since 2020. We host an annual fundraising dinner where all profits are donated; the 2024 event raised just over £3,200.
- Local schools: I have delivered talks and simple cooking demos at three local primary schools (Worksop Priory, Redlands, and St. Anne’s) to encourage children to try seasonal vegetables and understand where food comes from.
- Community Garden Collaboration: We support a community garden project off Kilton Hill by providing compost, buying their surplus herbs and salad leaves, and hosting their volunteer appreciation lunch once a year free of charge.

Professional Networks:
- Member of the Nottinghamshire Independent Hospitality Network.
- Informal mentor to two new café owners in Retford and Mansfield, offering advice on menu costing, supplier relationships, and staff wellbeing.

All of the above demonstrates that my work at The Green Fork Bistro is not just about running a successful restaurant, but about using food and drink as a vehicle for positive change in North Notts – socially, environmentally, and economically.
